1.name: MeToo
1.outcome_summary: Major cultural shift plus substantive policy wins on NDAs and workplace harassment law, but structural power in entertainment, media, and politics largely intact. Weinstein's NY conviction overturned in 2024. Time's Up shut down in 2023. The cultural conversation was permanently altered; the power structures were not.

progress_markers.event: Tarana Burke coins 'Me Too' on Myspace, focused on young Black and brown survivors in low-income communities
Dylan Farrow's open letter about Woody Allen; Cosby accusers speak publicly — momentum builds with no institutional response
Access Hollywood tape demonstrates elite impunity; Roger Ailes forced from Fox News after harassment settlements revealed
Ronan Farrow/NYT Weinstein exposé Oct 5-10; Alyssa Milano's tweet Oct 15 — 19 million uses of hashtag in 24 hours
Dozens of high-profile firings within weeks: Matt Lauer (NBC), Charlie Rose (CBS), Louis C.K., Kevin Spacey, Al Franken
Time's Up legal defense fund launched Jan 1; $24M raised for low-income survivors' legal costs
12+ US states pass NDA reform laws restricting secret settlements in harassment cases
Aziz Ansari and Junot Diaz cases generate 'witch hunt' and 'due process' counter-narrative; movement fragmentation
Harvey Weinstein convicted of rape and sexual assault in New York; sentenced to 23 years
Speak Out Act passes US Congress restricting NDAs in workplace sexual harassment cases
Ending Forced Arbitration of Sexual Assault and Sexual Harassment Act signed — survivors can access courts
Time's Up foundation shuts down after leadership scandals and financial mismanagement revelations
Weinstein convicted in Los Angeles on additional rape charges, sentenced to 16 years
New York Court of Appeals overturns Weinstein NY conviction on grounds of improper testimony; retrial ordered

lead_orgs.description: Original org, community-based, focused on Black and brown survivors; Burke reclaimed leadership after 2017

lead_orgs.source_note: RAINN official about page; role in MeToo support widely documented

lead_orgs.source_note: ACLU women's rights advocacy page; ACLU supported NDA reform legislation aligned with MeToo but was not a primary lead org

funders.source_note: Ford Foundation co-led $20M workplace sexual harassment fund, documented 2019

funders.source_note: MacArthur funds social justice; not confirmed as direct MeToo movement funder though aligned with movement goals

villains: Fox News (Roger Ailes/Bill O'Reilly NDA culture)
allies: Multiple studios adopting mandatory harassment training

international.name: UN Women
international.source_note: UN Women Executive Director issued statement on MeToo as political moment and tracked legislation internationally, October 2017

international.name: ILO Convention 190 (2019)
international.source_note: ILO Convention 190 on violence at work adopted 2019; widely linked to MeToo momentum

international.name: Amnesty International
international.source_note: Amnesty International documented human rights dimensions of MeToo and advocated for accountability

international.name: United Nations
international.source_note: Various UN agencies published reports tracking MeToo impact; broad institutional monitoring role implied

1.what_actually_changed: The US legal landscape on workplace harassment has materially changed: NDAs can no longer silence sexual harassment victims in many states, forced arbitration clauses are unenforceable for sexual assault claims, and dozens of serial predators lost careers. However, structural power in entertainment and media institutions was not dismantled — Hollywood leadership remains predominantly male, and Time's Up collapsed by 2023. Weinstein's New York conviction being overturned represents the limits of criminal accountability even in the highest-profile case.

cross_movement_links.relationship: Global feminist wave; bodily autonomy framing

2.name: Black Lives Matter — 2020 Tipping
2.outcome_summary: The largest protest movement in American history generated enormous institutional pledges and some policy changes, but most concrete policing reforms were reversed by 2021-2022. BLMGNF financial scandals damaged the organization. Cultural shift is permanent and generational; structural change to policing is minimal. The 2024 election saw backlash politics partly fueled by anti-BLM sentiment.

progress_markers.event: BLM founded by Patrisse Cullors, Alicia Garza, Opal Tometi after Trayvon Martin acquittal
Michael Brown (Ferguson) and Eric Garner killings; Ferguson uprising; BLM chapter network begins building in 30+ cities
Alton Sterling and Philando Castile killings; Colin Kaepernick kneels during NFL anthem — broadens cultural front
COVID reveals racial health disparities; Breonna Taylor killed March 13; Ahmaud Arbery killed Feb 23
George Floyd killed May 25; bystander video of Derek Chauvin kneeling on neck for 8:46 min; protests in all 50 states within days
15-26 million US participants; 4,000+ protests in 60 countries; largest protest movement in US history
$10.6 billion pledged to racial justice orgs by major corporations and foundations
Minneapolis City Council voted to defund police; Portland, NYC, LA cut budgets; dozens of cities adopted reform measures
Confederate statues removed across US and UK (Cecil Rhodes, Edward Colston in Bristol)
Derek Chauvin convicted of murder April 20 — rare conviction of a police officer
Minneapolis voters reject ballot measure to replace police department Nov 2021; most 'defund' measures reversed
BLMGNF financial scandal: Patrisse Cullors accused of misusing $6M in funds; resignations and factional splits
SCOTUS ends affirmative action; DEI programs rolled back at major corporations under political pressure
Trump administration executive orders ban DEI in federal agencies; private sector follows; many police reform laws repealed

international.source_note: UN Human Rights Chief Bachelet called for independent investigation into George Floyd killing, June 2020

international.source_note: UN OHCHR and Human Rights Council engaged with racial justice issues during 2020 protests

2.what_actually_changed: Derek Chauvin's conviction set a precedent for police accountability, and some jurisdictions passed meaningful reform laws (Minnesota, Colorado, California). The cultural conversation around systemic racism reached mainstream audiences at unprecedented scale, and 'police abolition' entered the policy debate. However, most 'defund' measures were reversed within 18 months, the George Floyd Justice in Policing Act never passed the Senate, BLMGNF's financial scandals damaged credibility, and the subsequent backlash contributed to the 2024 rightward political shift. The generational consciousness change is real; the structural change to American policing is minimal.

3.name: Plastic Straw / Single-Use Plastic Movement
3.outcome_summary: Major corporate and legislative action on single-use plastics globally — 70+ countries banned plastic straws and/or bags. EU Single-Use Plastics Directive 2021 is comprehensive legislation. But straws represent approximately 0.025% of ocean plastic by weight; the narrative win exceeded the material win. The global plastics treaty collapsed in 2024. Ocean plastic levels still rising.

progress_markers.event: Captain Charles Moore discovers Great Pacific Garbage Patch; ocean plastic enters scientific mainstream
California first US state to ban single-use plastic bags; demonstrates legislative viability
Marine biologist Christine Figgener's video removing plastic straw from sea turtle nostril goes viral — 28M+ views; emotional trigger crystallized
Break Free From Plastic coalition formed globally; 1,300+ organizations; NGO infrastructure ready to convert attention to policy
UK PM Theresa May announces intention to eliminate avoidable plastic waste; Scotland announces ban
Starbucks pledges to eliminate plastic straws by 2020; McDonald's follows; corporate pledge race begins
Seattle and San Francisco become first major US cities to ban plastic straws
EU Single-Use Plastics Directive adopted — bans 10 most common single-use plastic items
Disability advocates object that straw bans harm people who need flexible plastic straws; exemptions required, complicating enforcement
EU ban enters force; 70+ countries have some form of single-use plastic legislation
UNEA resolution to negotiate global plastics treaty — first potential legally binding international instrument
Starbucks paper straws fail; company abandons for strawless lids; consumer frustration with alternatives undermines narrative
Global Plastics Treaty negotiations collapse in Busan, South Korea — oil-producing nations block binding reduction targets
Treaty negotiations continue; ocean plastic levels still rising despite bans; upstream production not addressed

3.what_actually_changed: Plastic straw bans exist in 70+ countries and thousands of cities, and the EU Single-Use Plastics Directive is genuine binding legislation covering 10 product categories. Consumer consciousness about single-use plastic has fundamentally shifted, and 'single-use' is now a regulated legal category. However, straws are less than 0.025% of ocean plastic by weight, the global plastics treaty collapsed in 2024, and ocean plastic levels continue rising because the campaign was more effective at banning visible consumer items than addressing upstream industrial production. A massive narrative win with modest material impact.

4.name: Fridays for Future
4.outcome_summary: The largest climate mobilization in history and a generation of youth climate activists normalized as political actors. 2,000+ climate emergency declarations. The EU Green Deal and US Inflation Reduction Act were partly driven by movement pressure. But no major new international climate deal emerged, post-COVID momentum stalled significantly, fossil fuel production increased globally 2023-2024.

progress_markers.event: IPCC 1.5°C Special Report published October 2018 — 12-year deadline for action enters mainstream
Greta Thunberg's solo strike outside Swedish parliament August 20 2018 — 'Skolstrejk for klimatet' sign
Greta's COP24 Katowice speech goes viral; movement spreads internationally via social media
March 15 2019: first global student climate strike — 1.4 million students in 100+ countries
May 2019: climate emergency declarations spread — UK parliament first national parliament to declare
September 20 2019: 6 million people globally in climate strikes — largest climate mobilization in history
November 2019: European Green Deal announced by new von der Leyen Commission — partly credited to FFF pressure
COP25 Madrid: movement pressure visible but no major new commitments; coal and oil nations block progress
COVID-19 ends mass street mobilization; online protests have fraction of impact; movement loses momentum
COP26 Glasgow: 1.5°C commitment formalized, coal phase-out agreed in principle; fossil fuel subsidies continue
US Inflation Reduction Act passes — $369B in climate spending, largest US climate investment in history
Ukraine war triggers European gas/coal revival; energy security trumps climate in political discourse
COP28 Dubai hosted by UAE oil exec Sultan Al Jaber; 'phaseout' of fossil fuels watered to 'transition away from'
FFF continues strikes at reduced scale; Greta pivots to Gaza solidarity; movement aging into political mainstream

4.what_actually_changed: FFF normalized youth as a political actor on climate, accelerated climate emergency declarations in 2,000+ jurisdictions, and contributed to the European Green Deal and US Inflation Reduction Act — substantive policy changes. The framing of climate action as intergenerational justice has permanently shifted media discourse. However, global fossil fuel production grew through 2023, emissions hit record highs, and no binding treaty emerged from the movement's peak years. The global strike format lost effectiveness post-COVID and hasn't recovered its 2019 scale.

5.name: Standing Rock / NoDAPL
5.outcome_summary: Won a short-term halt under Obama but the pipeline was approved by Trump 4 days into his presidency and has been operational since 2017. The movement produced lasting cultural effects — water is life became a global indigenous rights slogan, land back movement accelerated, indigenous-environmental alliances deepened — but failed in its primary material objective. Pipeline still flows.

progress_markers.event: Iowa farmers and Meskwaki Nation file first objections to DAPL route; legal fights begin
Army Corps routes pipeline near Standing Rock after Bismarck objects to original route — tribe learns of rerouting
Standing Rock Sioux file lawsuit; Sacred Stone Camp established April 2016 by LaDonna Brave Bull Allard
September 3-4 2016: private security dogs attack water protectors on camera; civil rights era resonance
October-November 2016: 300+ tribal nations gather — largest indigenous gathering in a century
November 2016: military veterans stand with Standing Rock; shields imagery goes globally viral
December 4 2016: Obama administration halts pipeline pending Environmental Impact Statement review
January 24 2017: Trump signs executive order approving Dakota Access Pipeline — 4 days into presidency
February 2017: protectors forcibly evicted from Oceti Sakowin camp; arrests; pipeline construction continues
April 2017: DAPL becomes operational; oil flowing under Missouri River
DC federal court orders EIS conducted; pipeline temporarily ordered shut down July 2020
DC Circuit Court reinstates pipeline; Biden administration does not stop it despite campaign promises
Keystone XL pipeline cancelled (Jan 2021 Biden executive order) — NoDAPL energy contributed to this win
Land back movement and indigenous rights consciousness significantly elevated; tribal consultation requirements strengthened in some states
DAPL still operational; Standing Rock Sioux water rights case continues; pipeline has had several oil spills

5.what_actually_changed: The Dakota Access Pipeline has been operational since April 2017 and the Standing Rock Sioux's primary demand was not achieved. However, the movement permanently elevated the land back movement, made 'Mni Wiconi' (water is life) a global slogan, accelerated indigenous-environmental alliance-building, and contributed to Keystone XL's cancellation. The Biden administration's failure to stop DAPL despite promises is a key lesson: electoral victories don't automatically translate to movement wins. Court challenges continue but the pipeline keeps flowing.

6.name: Marriage Equality — The Long Arc
6.outcome_summary: Full legal victory at the Supreme Court in 2015, sustained by Respect for Marriage Act in 2022. Public support went from 27% (1996) to 70%+ (2024). Gold standard for deliberate narrative engineering producing cultural tipping before legal tipping. Currently vulnerable to SCOTUS reversal if Obergefell is reconsidered, but the 2022 federal statute provides additional protection.

progress_markers.event: Hawaii Supreme Court rules ban on same-sex marriage may be discriminatory — first legal opening; triggers national backlash
DOMA (Defense of Marriage Act) signed by Clinton; federal definition of marriage as one man, one woman
Vermont creates civil unions — first legal recognition; 'separate but equal' compromise
Massachusetts becomes first US state to legalize same-sex marriage (Goodridge v. Dept of Public Health)
11 state constitutional amendments banning same-sex marriage pass on Election Day — Karl Rove uses as mobilization tool
California Prop 8 passes (52-48), banning same-sex marriage after brief legalization — $100M campaign on both sides
Public support crosses 50% majority for first time; New York legalizes same-sex marriage
Obama becomes first sitting president to support marriage equality; 3 states approve at ballot
SCOTUS Windsor decision strikes down DOMA; Prop 8 dismissed on standing; California marriages resume
Obergefell v. Hodges (June 26 2015): SCOTUS rules nationwide marriage equality in 5-4 decision
Respect for Marriage Act passes Congress with bipartisan support; provides federal backup if Obergefell overturned
SCOTUS justices Thomas and Alito signal openness to reconsidering Obergefell; movement remains vigilant on trans rights rollbacks

6.what_actually_changed: Same-sex couples can marry in all 50 US states, access federal marriage benefits, and are protected by the Respect for Marriage Act even if Obergefell is reconsidered. Public support went from 27% to 70%+ in three decades — one of the fastest opinion shifts in polling history. The movement demonstrated that a 30-year strategic campaign can transform majoritarian opinion from opposition to support. The remaining gap: marriage equality has not translated to full non-discrimination protections in all states, and trans rights have faced significant legislative rollback since 2021.

7.name: Chile Estallido Social
7.outcome_summary: A historic constitutional process was launched but failed twice — both new constitutions rejected by referendum. Gabriel Boric elected president. The 1980 Pinochet constitution remains in effect. Minor social concessions. Political landscape permanently altered but the neoliberal structural model — privatized water, health, education, pensions — not dismantled.

progress_markers.event: 'Penguin Revolution' — high school students protest education system; first major youth uprising of this generation
Massive student protests for free public education; Gabriel Boric and Camila Vallejo emerge as student leaders
No+AFP (anti-privatized pension) movement organizes; demonstrates organizational capacity
Student 'evasion' fare-jump protests grow for weeks; CPI shows mounting inequality; multiple sectors simultaneously aggrieved
October 18 2019: subway fare increase of 30 pesos ($0.04); state of emergency; military in streets for first time since Pinochet
October 25: one million people march in Santiago — largest demonstration in Chilean history
Las Tesis feminist collective performs 'Un Violador en tu Camino' — goes globally viral; performed in 50+ countries
Police violence: 7,000+ injured, 2,500 arrested, 30+ killed, hundreds with eye injuries from rubber bullets
November 15: 'Acuerdo por la Paz Social y la Nueva Constitucion' — agreement for constitutional referendum
October 25 2020 plebiscite: 78% vote for new constitution; 79% vote for elected constitutional convention
Gabriel Boric elected president November 2021 — former student leader, youngest president in Chilean history
September 4 2022: first new constitution rejected in referendum — 62% against, 38% for
December 17 2023: second constitutional draft rejected — 55% against; process ends; Pinochet constitution remains
Boric government continues reform efforts within existing constitution; movement energy dissipated without constitutional victory

7.what_actually_changed: The 1980 Pinochet constitution remains in effect after two failed referendum processes — the movement's core failure. However, Chile elected its youngest president in history (Boric, a movement figure), demonstrated that mass mobilization can force constitutional conversations previously unthinkable, and produced 'Un Violador en tu Camino' as a globally replicated feminist tool. Minor social policy improvements occurred but the structural neoliberal model has not been dismantled. The political landscape is permanently altered; the system is not.

8.name: EndSARS Nigeria
8.outcome_summary: SARS officially disbanded within 8 days — a nominal victory. But SWAT replaced it with similar practices, the Lekki Toll Gate massacre killed an unknown number of protesters, no soldiers were held accountable, and structural police brutality continues. Most durable legacy: Nigerian youth political awakening that shaped 2023 elections.

progress_markers.event: SARS established; begins targeting young men for extortion and 'looking criminal' (laptops, dreadlocks)
#EndSARS hashtag first appears; Segun Awosanya begins systematic documentation of abuses
First government promise to reform SARS — ignored; pattern repeats in 2019
COVID creates unemployed youth with nothing to lose; SARS abuses accelerate during lockdowns
October 3 2020: video of SARS killing young man in Delta State goes viral on Twitter within hours
October 8-11: spontaneous nationwide protests; no central organization; Twitter coordination via #EndSARS
October 11: Nigerian Police Force officially disbands SARS nationwide — fastest capitulation in recent Nigerian history
Feminist Coalition raises 150M+ naira ($400K) in 72 hours via crowdfunding for protest logistics and injured protesters
October 14: SWAT announced as SARS replacement — widely seen as rebranding without reform
October 20: Lekki Toll Gate massacre — military opens fire on peaceful protesters; 12-60+ killed
October 21: nationwide curfew; movement suppressed by force; organizers arrested
Lagos judicial panel confirms Lekki killings; government ordered to pay compensation — largely unpaid
Peter Obi / Labour Party surge in presidential election driven by EndSARS generation — political awakening affects electoral landscape

8.what_actually_changed: SARS was officially disbanded — a nominal victory. But SWAT replicated SARS practices, no officers were held accountable for Lekki, and structural police brutality in Nigeria has not materially changed. The most durable legacy is political: a generation of Nigerian youth who organized outside traditional party structures developed digital activism infrastructure and in 2023 nearly elected Peter Obi as an outsider presidential candidate through the same organizational methods. The Feminist Coalition model of anonymous, tech-enabled movement finance is a lasting innovation.

sources.title: Academic analysis of Nigerian youth activism

9.name: India Farmers' Protest
9.outcome_summary: Complete victory: PM Modi announced repeal of all three Farm Laws on national television November 19, 2021 — 13 months into the protest. One of the most significant democratic reversals in Indian history. However, the MSP guarantee was promised but not legislated. A 2024 follow-up protest demanding legal MSP confirms the secondary demand remains unfulfilled.

progress_markers.event: Farmer debt crisis deepens; 300,000+ farmer suicides documented 1995-2020; structural agrarian crisis established
September 2020: Three Farm Acts passed by Parliament in 24 hours with no public consultation — shock to farming communities
October-November 2020: Punjab farmers organize; tractor rallies; state-level mobilization builds
November 26-27 2020: 'Dilli Chalo' — farmers march on Delhi; police use tear gas and water cannons; farmers block highways
December 2020: Samyukt Kisan Morcha coalition of 40+ unions forms with unified demands
January 26 2021: Tractor parade turns chaotic; some protesters breach Red Fort; government uses incident to delegitimize movement
February 2021: Rihanna and Greta tweet support; Indian government's nationalist counter-response amplifies movement globally
Internet shutdowns at protest sites; sedition charges filed against journalist Disha Ravi for sharing toolkit
October 2021: Lakhimpur Kheri violence — BJP minister's son allegedly runs over farmers; 4 killed
November 19 2021: PM Modi announces repeal of all three Farm Acts on national TV — unprecedented capitulation
December 2021: farmers end 13-month sit-in after government commits to MSP committee
Punjab state elections: BJP loses; AAP sweeps — movement's political effect visible
Second farmers' protest begins February 2024 demanding legal MSP guarantee; blocked at Shambhu border

9.what_actually_changed: All three Farm Acts were repealed in November 2021 — a complete and historic victory on the primary demand. The laws literally ceased to exist, and the government that passed them publicly capitulated. However, the MSP guarantee (minimum support price legally mandated) was promised in committee form but never legislated, meaning farmers' income security remains structurally vulnerable. The 2024 follow-up protest demonstrates the incomplete nature of the win. The movement proved that 13 months of disciplined physical presence can overcome one of the most popular governments in Indian history.

sources.title: Academic analysis of Indian agrarian movements

10.name: South Korea Candlelight Revolution
10.outcome_summary: Complete success: President Park Geun-hye impeached by parliament December 2016, upheld by Constitutional Court March 2017, arrested and convicted, Moon Jae-in elected. The movement achieved every goal through peaceful democratic means. One of the cleanest examples of mass nonviolent democratic accountability in modern history. The 2024 Yoon Suk-yeol martial law episode showed the model still works.

progress_markers.event: Sewol ferry disaster kills 304 mostly students; government response delayed and dishonest; trust in Park collapses
Park approval ratings decline; corruption allegations accumulate; 'blacklist' of critical artists exposed
'Hell Joseon' discourse peaks among youth; economic discontent; democratic backsliding perceived
October 24-29: JTBC broadcaster reveals Choi Soon-sil possessed and edited classified presidential speeches and policy documents
October 29: First candlelight vigil — 30,000 people in Gwanghwamun Square
November 5: Second vigil — 200,000 people; multigenerational, peaceful, families with children
November 12: Third vigil — 1 million in Seoul, 2 million nationwide; largest protest since democratization
November 26: 1.9 million nationwide; Park approval rating hits 4%
December 9: National Assembly votes to impeach Park 234-56; suspended from duties
March 10: Constitutional Court upholds impeachment unanimously 8-0; Park removed from office
April: Park arrested on corruption and abuse of power charges
May 9: Moon Jae-in elected president — first liberal president in 10 years
Park convicted and sentenced to 25 years (later reduced); Samsung's Lee Jae-yong also convicted
President Yoon declares martial law December 3 2024; rapidly reversed; National Assembly impeaches Yoon December 14 — model works again

international.source_note: UN OHCHR monitors South Korea; no documented specific UN engagement with candlelight protests

10.what_actually_changed: President Park was removed from office, convicted, and imprisoned. The constitutional process worked as designed. Samsung's Lee Jae-yong was convicted of bribery and served time (later pardoned — ongoing controversy). The 2024 Yoon martial law episode confirmed the model is durable: civil society and parliament rapidly removed an overreaching executive again. South Korea demonstrated that a peaceful, aesthetically compelling mass mobilization can hold even the most powerful president accountable through legal democratic mechanisms — and that the model can be replicated within the same political system less than a decade later.

sources.title: Academic analysis of Korean democracy movements

11.name: Argentina Abortion / Green Wave
11.outcome_summary: Full legislative victory: Argentina legalized abortion on demand up to 14 weeks (Law 27,610) on December 30, 2020, entering force January 14, 2021. The Green Wave visual template spread across Latin America and directly influenced Mexico (2021), Colombia (2022). Law remains in effect despite Milei opposition.

progress_markers.event: NiUnaMenos (June 3 2015) establishes feminist coalition infrastructure; organizational base for abortion campaign
Campana Nacional por el Derecho al Aborto Legal forms; annual bill presentations begin; green handkerchief adopted as symbol
First abortion bill reaches Senate floor: 'Green tide' — massive street mobilization; Senate votes June-August 2018
August 9 2018: Senate rejects bill 38-31; protesters weep but immediately recommit — defeat transformed into momentum
Alberto Fernandez elected president on platform including abortion rights — political pathway opens
November 2020: Fernandez government submits abortion bill to parliament with presidential support
December 11 2020: Chamber of Deputies passes 131-117; green handkerchiefs wave in the rain outside parliament
December 30 2020: Senate passes 38-29; Law 27,610 signed — abortion legal to 14 weeks
September 2021: Mexico Supreme Court rules unanimously that criminalization of abortion is unconstitutional — Green Wave direct influence
February 2022: Colombia Constitutional Court legalizes abortion up to 24 weeks — Green Wave influence cited
US Dobbs decision overturning Roe v. Wade creates global backlash; Green Wave becomes symbol of resistance
Javier Milei elected president November 2023; vows to roll back abortion law; has not succeeded legislatively
Law remains in effect; implementation uneven in conservative provinces; regional movement continues expanding

11.what_actually_changed: Argentina legalized abortion up to 14 weeks of pregnancy — a complete and binding legislative victory. The law is in effect and funded through the public health system (though implementation varies by province due to conscientious objector provisions). The visual template — green handkerchief, 'panuelezo' mass wave — became the signal for feminist movements from Mexico to the Philippines. Mexico decriminalized at the federal level, Colombia expanded access to 24 weeks. The Milei government opposes the law but has not been able to repeal it.

slogans: Educacion sexual para decidir, anticonceptivos para no abortar, aborto legal para no morir
key_media: Documentary: Que Sea Ley (Let It Be Law, 2019)

sources.title: Academic analysis of reproductive rights movements

12.name: NiUnaMenos — Anti-Femicide
12.outcome_summary: Created the framework for femicide as a named systemic crime. Influenced legislation across Latin America. Generated global feminist organizing language. Built the infrastructure that won the Green Wave abortion victory. Femicide rates have not dramatically declined — the awareness and framing win is real; the structural violence continues.

progress_markers.event: Susana Chavez, Mexican poet who coined 'Ni Una Menos,' murdered in Juarez — slogan becomes memorial to its own author
Chiara Paez (14, pregnant) found buried in boyfriend's backyard in Argentina — case galvanizes feminist networks
June 3 2015: First NiUnaMenos march — 200,000+ in Buenos Aires and 80 cities; journalists, artists, activists organized in 10 days
Argentina's 2012 femicide law gains enforcement focus; femicide as distinct crime category enters legal/media vocabulary
Movement spreads: Chile, Mexico, Uruguay, Peru, Bolivia all hold NiUnaMenos marches within weeks
National strike (paro nacional de mujeres) in Argentina October 19 2016 — first women's strike against femicide
International Women's Strike March 8 2017 — NiUnaMenos contributes to global feminist resurgence parallel to MeToo
Mexico NiUnaMenos march after spate of femicides; 'glitter protest' at CNDH — police chief glitter-bombed
Argentine National Femicide Register publishes annual data — making the pattern visible through official statistics
Movement organizational infrastructure directly enables Green Wave abortion law victory
Femicide rates in Argentina: 2020=298, 2021=295, 2022=280 — declining slightly but not dramatically
Movement continues; March 8 marches in Latin America remain some of world's largest feminist mobilizations

12.what_actually_changed: Femicide is now legally recognized as a distinct crime category across most of Latin America, with enhanced penalties. Official femicide registries exist in multiple countries, making the pattern visible through data. Media coverage of gender-based killings has fundamentally shifted from 'domestic tragedy' to systemic analysis. However, femicide rates have not dramatically declined — between 200-300 women are killed in Argentina alone annually. The movement created the cultural and organizational infrastructure for the Green Wave, its most tangible policy legacy.

slogans: Ni una menos (Not one woman less)
Vivas nos queremos (We want to live)
Si tocan a una, respondemos todas
key_media: Documentary: Que Sea Ley (Let It Be Law)

sources.title: Academic analysis of Latin American feminism

13.name: Fees Must Fall
13.outcome_summary: Fee-free higher education for students from families earning under R350,000/year introduced in 2018 — a significant policy win. But the 2015 zero fee increase was only a one-year victory; decolonization demands were largely unmet; NSFAS implementation is chronically underfunded; the 'missing middle' problem persists. Leaders faced criminal charges.

Government deploys private security on campuses; hundreds arrested; movement facing criticism for divisive tactics
December 2017: Zuma announces fee-free higher education for students from families earning under R350,000/year
NSFAS (student loans) expanded to cover more students; implementation begins
NSFAS implementation plagued by administrative failures; many students excluded; 'missing middle' problem remains
Movement leaders face criminal charges; institutional decolonization remains mainly symbolic (curriculum changes, some statue removals)
NSFAS remains underfunded and administratively dysfunctional; free education policy exists but delivery is inconsistent

13.what_actually_changed: Fee-free higher education for students from low-income households is now policy — a genuine change that directly benefits hundreds of thousands of students annually. Some statues removed, some curriculum decolonization. However, the 'missing middle' remains a problem, NSFAS implementation is chronically underfunded and badly administered, and university culture and curriculum remain largely unchanged from apartheid-era patterns. The decolonization agenda is ongoing without institutional resolution.

sources.title: Academic analysis of South African student movements

14.name: Sudan Revolution
14.outcome_summary: Al-Bashir's 30-year rule ended April 2019. For approximately 2 years there was a civilian-led transitional government. Then a 2021 military coup reversed the democratic transition. The subsequent SAF-RSF civil war (April 2023) has caused catastrophic humanitarian disaster — 8M+ displaced, world's largest displacement crisis. The revolution succeeded in removing Bashir and led to a civil war.

progress_markers.event: Omar al-Bashir seizes power in military coup; 30-year Islamist authoritarian rule begins
ICC issues arrest warrant for al-Bashir for genocide in Darfur; regime internationally isolated
Bread and fuel prices triple; economy in freefall; Sudanese Professional Association begins organizing
December 19 2018: Protests begin in Atbara after bread price tripling; security kills protesters; spreads to Khartoum
April 6 2019: Massive sit-in begins outside military HQ — city within a city with library, clinic, music stage
April 11 2019: Military removes and arrests al-Bashir — ending 30-year rule
June 3 2019: RSF (Rapid Support Forces) massacres sit-in protesters — 60-128+ killed, hundreds raped, thousands injured
July-August 2019: International pressure secures civilian-military 'Sovereignty Council' power-sharing deal
Abdalla Hamdok (civilian economist) appointed PM; democratic transition begins; ICC cooperation on Bashir
October 25 2021: Military coup by Burhan and Hemeti; Hamdok arrested; democratic transition ended
Hamdok resigned January 2022; protests suppressed; activists arrested and tortured
April 15 2023: SAF-RSF civil war breaks out; Khartoum becomes war zone; 8M+ displaced
Civil war continues; RSF commits atrocities in Darfur; revolution's gains entirely erased by catastrophic conflict

14.what_actually_changed: Al-Bashir's 30-year dictatorship ended — a genuine historic achievement. For approximately 2 years Sudan had a civilian-led transitional government pursuing democratic institutions. All of this was reversed by the 2021 coup and then catastrophically destroyed by the 2023 civil war, producing one of the world's worst humanitarian crises. Sudan in 2025 is in a dramatically worse humanitarian and political state than in 2018. The revolution succeeded in removing Bashir and failed to prevent what followed.

key_media: Documentary: Sudan: A History of Violence

15.name: Malawi Election Protests
15.outcome_summary: Complete legal and democratic victory: Constitutional Court annulled the fraudulent 2019 election, fresh elections held June 2020, incumbent Mutharika lost, Chakwera inaugurated. First time in African history a court annulled a presidential election and the outcome was peacefully accepted. A landmark continental democratic precedent.

progress_markers.event: Peter Mutharika elected president; corruption allegations and governance concerns begin accumulating
Human Rights Defenders Coalition (HRDC) active; civil society monitoring institutions strengthening
May 21 2019: Elections held; MEC announces Mutharika wins; immediate concerns from observers about irregularities
Evidence emerges: ballot tallying sheets show tipp-ex corrections; photographs circulate on social media — undeniable physical evidence
Chakwera and Chilima challenge election in Constitutional Court; HRDC organizes sustained street protests
Massive sustained protests in Blantyre and Lilongwe; tear gas, clashes — civil society maintains pressure for months
MEC Chairwoman Jane Ansah resigns under pressure — first institutional accountability
February 3 2020: Constitutional Court annuls 2019 election unanimously — landmark ruling citing widespread fraud including tipp-ex alterations
Malawi Electoral Commission reconstituted with new leadership
June 23 2020: Fresh elections held; Lazarus Chakwera wins 58.57% — clean result accepted by all parties
June 28 2020: Mutharika accepts results; Chakwera inaugurated — peaceful democratic transition
SADC election observation praises process; becomes continental precedent for democratic accountability
Chakwera government faces corruption allegations — democratic process intact but governance disappoints

15.what_actually_changed: A fraudulent election was legally annulled, a new election was held, the incumbent lost and accepted the result, and a new president was peacefully inaugurated. The Malawi Constitutional Court ruling is a continental landmark — referenced in subsequent African election disputes. Civil society sustained pressure plus independent judiciary can work even against an incumbent who controls state resources. Long-term: democratic norms strengthened; governance quality of new government is debated but the democratic process was preserved.

slogans: Tippex will not silence our vote

16.name: Zimbabwe — Fall of Mugabe
16.outcome_summary: Robert Mugabe's 37-year rule ended November 2017. But the removal was primarily a military/elite operation enabled by popular celebration, not a democratic revolution. Emmerson Mnangagwa has continued authoritarian governance. 2023 elections deeply flawed. Zimbabwe traded one ZANU-PF ruler for another. Economic crisis continues.

progress_markers.event: Farm seizures; economic collapse begins; Zimbabwe loses most of its economic value over following decade
Hyperinflation peaks at 89 sextillion percent; Z$100 trillion notes issued; economy completely destroyed
Unity government with MDC briefly stabilizes economy using foreign currencies; collapse averted temporarily
Mugabe, 93, visibly declining; wife Grace Mugabe (G40 faction) positions for succession; military (Lacoste faction) alarmed
November 6: Mugabe sacks VP Mnangagwa; military sees this as existential threat to their interests
November 14-15: Military takes over ZBC broadcaster; announces 'not a coup'; places Mugabe under house arrest
November 18: 100,000+ march in Harare celebrating military action; war veterans and civil society join
November 21: Mugabe resigns after parliament begins impeachment proceedings; nation celebrates
July 2018 elections: Mnangagwa wins narrowly; post-election protests met with military force; 6 killed
January 2019 fuel price protests; military cracks down; internet shut down; 12+ killed
COVID used as pretext to arrest journalists and opposition figures; Hopewell Chin'ono arrested
August 2023: Elections widely condemned as fraudulent; Mnangagwa declared winner; SADC observers cite irregularities
Economic crisis continues; brain drain; authoritarian rule entrenched; no meaningful democratic progress

16.what_actually_changed: Robert Mugabe's 37-year personal rule ended — a genuine historic milestone. However, ZANU-PF remains in power under Mnangagwa, who has continued authoritarian methods including election manipulation, press suppression, and using security forces against protesters. The economic model has not changed. There is no meaningful democratic progress. What changed: one old man left power. What didn't change: the system that sustained him.

key_media: Documentary: Mugabe and the White African (2009)

17.name: Green Belt Movement — Kenya
17.outcome_summary: 51 million trees planted, constitutional environmental rights secured, model replicated in 30+ countries, Nobel Peace Prize 2004. A 27-year movement built from zero that genuinely changed Kenya's landscape and governance and created an enduring international institution. One of the most complete grassroots environmental-democracy victories of the 20th century. Continues under new leadership.

progress_markers.event: Wangari Maathai founds Green Belt Movement; organizes rural women to plant trees; links environment to democracy and women's empowerment
Maathai challenges Moi regime; GBM declared subversive; organizers harassed and monitored
Maathai blocks Uhuru Park skyscraper development — trees saved; Moi government retreats under international pressure
Maathai beaten and arrested during protests for multiparty democracy; jailed briefly; movement continues
Pro-democracy hunger strike in Uhuru Park by mothers of political prisoners; succeeded in pressuring releases
30,000 women organized; 20 million trees planted; model being replicated internationally across Africa
End of Moi era; multi-party democracy restored; political space for movement expands dramatically
Wangari Maathai wins Nobel Peace Prize — first environmentalist, first African woman to receive Nobel Peace Prize
Maathai appointed Assistant Minister for Environment; 40 million trees planted; movement inside government
New Kenyan constitution includes environmental rights — movement's advocacy contributed to constitutional provision
Maathai dies September 25 2011; Green Belt Movement continues as permanent institution
51+ million trees planted since 1977; movement operating in 30+ African countries
Kenya's newly elected Ruto government announces massive tree-planting program — GBM legacy influences national policy

17.what_actually_changed: 51+ million trees planted — a genuine, verifiable, physical change to Kenya's landscape. Environmental rights in the 2010 Kenyan constitution. An enduring institution (GBM) that has operated continuously for 47 years. The Nobel Prize validated the model that environmental work, political corruption, and women's empowerment are a unified justice framework — a conceptual contribution that influenced environmental movements globally. Women in 30+ African countries organized through GBM-inspired models.

slogans: Until you dig a hole, plant a tree, water it and make it survive, you haven't done a thing
We are called to assist the earth to heal her wounds
When we plant trees, we plant the seeds of peace

sources.title: Academic analysis of women's environmental movements

18.name: Kenya Maandamano / Finance Bill Protests 2024
18.outcome_summary: Finance Bill 2024 fully withdrawn by President Ruto within 8 days — a rapid and complete capitulation on the primary demand. Cabinet reshuffled. Represents Gen Z's first major political victory via TikTok-organized movement in Africa — a new model of decentralized digital organizing with real-world policy consequences.

progress_markers.event: Ruto elected president on 'hustler economy' pro-poor platform; expectations of economic relief
Cost of living crisis deepens; Finance Bill 2023 includes harsh tax measures; smaller protests; precedent set
Finance Bill 2024 proposed: cooking oil tax, bread tax, mobile money tax — hitting daily survival costs
June 18 2024: Gen Z organizers call protest via TikTok; larger than expected; police use live ammunition — 3 killed, dozens wounded
June 25: Protesters breach parliament; parliament set alight; 13 killed overall in protest week; global media attention
June 26: President Ruto signs withdrawal of Finance Bill — complete reversal of flagship tax policy
July: Ruto dismisses most of his cabinet; announces national dialogue and new government formation
Ruto invites opposition into government; broad-based government formed — criticized as co-optation by some protesters
Subsequent protest demands (Ruto resignation, accountability) not met; some protest leaders arrested
Movement continues pressing accountability demands; new Finance Bill passed with fewer controversial measures

18.what_actually_changed: The Finance Bill 2024 was withdrawn — complete reversal of flagship government policy within 8 days. Ruto's cabinet was dismissed and reconstructed. The movement demonstrated that TikTok-era organizing, intentionally leaderless, can win material policy victories against sitting governments. Finance bill withdrawal saved Kenyan households from significant additional tax burden. Long-term structural change to Kenya's fiscal governance remains incomplete, but the precedent of rapid capitulation to youth protest pressure is established.

sources.title: Academic analysis of Kenyan youth politics

19.name: Burkina Faso Popular Uprising
19.outcome_summary: Compaoré's 27-year rule ended in 24 hours. A civilian government was elected and governed for 7 years. But by 2025 Burkina Faso is under military rule aligned with Russia, experiencing one of the world's worst jihadist insurgencies, with press suppressed and democracy suspended. The uprising succeeded in removing a dictator and failed entirely to build institutional foundations for democracy.

progress_markers.event: Blaise Compaore seizes power by assassinating Thomas Sankara; begins 27-year rule
Arab Spring inspires protests in Burkina Faso; Compaore makes concessions but maintains power
Balai Citoyen (Citizen's Broom) civil society movement founded by Smockey (rapper) and Serge Bambara; organizes resistance to constitutional amendment
October 30: Parliament session to vote on constitutional amendment; thousands storm parliament; building set on fire; soldiers pull back
October 31: Compaore flees to Ivory Coast within 24 hours; military announces transitional authority
Presidential guard (RSP) attempts coup September 2015; defeated; RSP dissolved
November 2015: Transition elections; Roch Marc Christian Kabore elected president — civilian government established
Jihadist attacks begin in north; Kabore government unable to control deteriorating security situation
Security catastrophically deteriorates; millions displaced; jihadists control large swaths of territory
January 24 2022: Military coup — Kabore removed; Lt. Col. Damiba takes power
September 30 2022: Second military coup — Capt. Ibrahim Traore removes Damiba
Traore government expels French forces; invites Wagner Group/Russia; suspends civilian press freedom
40%+ of territory under jihadist control; 2M+ displaced; democracy suspended; 2014 revolution's gains entirely erased

19.what_actually_changed: Compaore's 27-year rule ended in a single day. A civilian government was elected and governed for 7 years. This was the entire positive legacy. By 2025, Burkina Faso is under military rule, aligned with Russia, experiencing one of the world's worst jihadist insurgencies, with press suppressed and democracy suspended. The 2014 uprising succeeded in removing a dictator and failed entirely to build institutional foundations for democracy that could survive security crisis. A textbook case of revolutionary success without institutional consolidation.

key_media: Documentary: Thomas Sankara: The Upright Man

20.name: Ethiopia Oromo Protests / Abiy Ahmed Rise
20.outcome_summary: Protests forced sweeping reforms and produced Africa's youngest Nobel Peace Prize winner as PM. Abiy Ahmed released political prisoners, liberalized media, and negotiated Eritrea peace. However, the Tigray war (2020-2022) caused 500,000+ deaths. Oromo marginalization continues; ethnic federalism remains contested. Reform was real but partial.

progress_markers.event: 'Addis Ababa Integrated Development Master Plan' threatens Oromo farmland absorption without compensation
November 2015: student protests against master plan; security forces kill protesters; protests escalate across Oromia
Master Plan officially abandoned January 2016 — first major concession to protesters
October 2 2016: Irreecha festival massacre — security fires on crowd; 52+ killed in stampede and shooting
Irreecha massacre triggers nationwide uprising; international media; EPRDF under maximum pressure
State of emergency declared October 2016; internet shutdowns; mass arrests; 1,000+ detained
February 2018: PM Hailemariam Desalegn resigns — extraordinary concession to protest pressure
April 2018: Abiy Ahmed (Oromo reformer) becomes PM; releases 60,000 political prisoners; lifts state of emergency
October 2019: Abiy Ahmed wins Nobel Peace Prize for Eritrea peace deal
June 2020: murder of musician Hachalu Hundessa triggers Oromo protests; 239 killed in crackdown
November 2020: Tigray war begins; Abiy orders military offensive; atrocities documented by UN
Pretoria Agreement ends Tigray war November 2022; 500,000+ dead; Abiy's human rights record destroyed
Amhara conflict continues; Oromo Liberation Army active; ethnic tensions unresolved; Abiy governing increasingly autocratically

20.what_actually_changed: The EPRDF coalition that dominated Ethiopia for 27 years was transformed under protest pressure: Abiy Ahmed's reforms — releasing political prisoners, allowing diaspora opposition return, liberalizing media, making peace with Eritrea — were genuinely historic. But the Tigray war revealed these reforms as partial, and Abiy's government has been accused of serious human rights violations. Oromo land rights and political representation remain contested. The movement succeeded in changing who held power; it did not succeed in building accountable democratic institutions.

sources.title: Academic analysis of Ethiopian ethnic federalism

21.name: Ghana #FixTheCountry
21.outcome_summary: Limited structural change — protests occurred after successful challenge to police injunction, government announced some infrastructure investment, no major policy change. Primarily an awareness and discourse-shifting movement. Contributed to political climate that saw Mahama defeat Bawumia in December 2024 elections.

progress_markers.event: COVID exposes infrastructure gaps in Ghana; fuel prices rise; cost of living crisis develops
#FixTheCountry begins as social media commentary on infrastructure failures, corruption, and poor governance
May 2021: Heavy rains and flooding in Accra kills 6; hashtag trends nationally; protest proposed
Police obtain court injunction blocking protest; civil society challenges it — court battles attract more attention than the flood
Injunction successfully challenged; protests proceed legally — August 4 2021 protest in Accra
Government response: announces road and drainage projects but no structural change in accountability
Movement lacks organizational depth; protests relatively small given hashtag's national prominence
Ghana's economic crisis deepens; IMF bailout required; 'dumsor' (power cuts) return; movement discourse continues
Formal movement energy dissipated; economic crisis continues under Akufo-Addo government
December 2024: John Mahama (NDC) wins presidential election; partly driven by #FixTheCountry sentiment in electorate

21.what_actually_changed: No major structural policy change resulted. The court victory over the injunction is a procedural win for protest rights. The movement's primary lasting impact was contributing to a political discourse that held the Akufo-Addo government accountable through the 2024 elections, which NDC won partly on 'fix the economy' messaging. Ghana's economic crisis and infrastructure failures remain ongoing structural problems.

viral_moments: Youth leaders arrested then released after public outcry
key_media: Joy FM radio coverage and debates

sources.title: Academic analysis of Ghanaian youth activism

22.name: Morocco Hirak Rif Movement
22.outcome_summary: Nine months of sustained protests, some regional investment announcements, then mass trials and long prison sentences for leaders — Nasser Zefzafi sentenced to 20 years. No structural reform of the relationship between the Rif region and the Moroccan state. Movement effectively suppressed through legal prosecution of its leadership.

progress_markers.event: Rif rebellion crushed by King Hassan II; memory of state violence against Berber north embedded in regional identity
Arab Spring: February 20 Movement protests in Morocco; King Mohammed VI offers constitutional reforms widely seen as cosmetic
October 28 2016: Mouhcine Fikri, fish vendor, crushed to death in garbage truck trying to retrieve confiscated swordfish; video goes viral
Thousands protest regional neglect and police impunity in Al Hoceima and Rif cities within days
Sustained protests through winter; Hirak Rif movement formalizes under Nasser Zefzafi; 'Haqquna' (our right) chant sustained
Movement grows to tens of thousands; government announces $12B Rif development plan — inadequately implemented
May 26 2017: Zefzafi arrested after interrupting Friday prayer to address worshippers; mass arrests follow
June 2017: 25,000+ protest in Rabat and Casablanca in solidarity; government does not respond
June 2018: Mass trial; Zefzafi and 5 other leaders sentenced to 20 years; 47 activists total sentenced
Appeals uphold sentences; international criticism from Amnesty International, Human Rights Watch
Zefzafi and core leaders remain imprisoned; some prisoner releases; Rif structural underdevelopment continues
Morocco's 2030 World Cup investment focuses on major cities; Rif marginalization continues structurally

22.what_actually_changed: Some regional development investment was announced for the Rif region — inadequately implemented. Movement leaders remain imprisoned on 20-year sentences. No structural reform of Morocco's political system or Berber cultural rights. International pressure has not secured prisoner releases. The Fikri case created significant momentary awareness but without institutional protection — visual power alone could not protect the movement from patient state repression.

slogans: We want schools and hospitals, not prisons
viral_moments: Mohsin Fikri crushed to death in garbage truck
Mass arrests of activists including Nasser Zefzafi
key_media: Al Jazeera and BBC coverage of Rif protests

23.name: Mali Protests / IBK Resignation
23.outcome_summary: President IBK resigned following a military coup on August 18, 2020 — the nominal demand was met, but by the military. Mali has subsequently had two more coups, expelled French forces, invited Wagner Group, withdrawn from ECOWAS, and experienced catastrophic security deterioration. The movement that removed IBK set the conditions for arguably worse governance.

progress_markers.event: Tuareg rebellion and jihadist seizure of northern Mali; French intervention saves the south; state credibility damaged
IBK elected president on anti-corruption platform; promises not delivered; security deteriorates
March 2020 legislative elections marred by fraud; Supreme Court reinstates contested results; June 5 Movement forms
June 5 2020: Massive protests in Bamako; Imam Dicko leads diverse coalition of secular and religious groups
July 10-11: Protesters attack and briefly occupy parliament and state TV — government in crisis
ECOWAS mediates; IBK offers national unity government — protesters reject, demand full resignation
August 18 2020: Military coup removes IBK; CNSP junta takes power; protesters celebrate — military, not civilians, now govern
May 2021: Coup within coup — Colonel Assimi Goita removes transitional civilian president; fully military government
French Operation Barkhane expelled; Wagner Group invited; ECOWAS imposes sanctions
ECOWAS and UN peacekeeping mission (MINUSMA) expelled; Mali isolated internationally
Mali withdraws from ECOWAS along with Burkina Faso and Niger; jihadist insurgency controls large territories
Goita government continues military rule; no civilian transition timeline; Wagner/Russia entrenched

23.what_actually_changed: IBK's government was removed — the stated demand. But democracy was not the outcome. Mali in 2025 is under military rule, aligned with Russia, experiencing a worse jihadist insurgency, isolated from regional institutions, and with no civilian democratic transition in sight. Imam Dicko, who helped lead the movement, became politically marginalized after the coup. The movement's success in removing IBK directly enabled the conditions that led to three successive military governments and regional isolation. Net outcome score: 0.

24.name: South Africa Marikana — Platinum Belt Massacre
24.outcome_summary: 34 miners killed by state police in democratic South Africa. Workers received a partial wage increase. No police were criminally convicted. ANC's moral authority among Black working-class South Africans severely damaged. Economic Freedom Fighters founded on the footage. Lonmin closed. Massacre is a lasting symbol of post-apartheid inequality's brutality.

progress_markers.event: Post-apartheid South Africa: racial wage gap persists in mining; rock drillers — most dangerous job — still earning poverty wages
Julius Malema rises in ANC Youth League; begins articulating economic transformation beyond Rainbow Nation narrative
Platinum belt wildcat strikes spread; workers reject NUM as too cozy with ANC; AMCU growing as alternative union
Lonmin workers demand wage increase from R4,000 to R12,500/month; management refuses; strike enters second week
August 16 2012: South African police open fire on striking miners; 34 killed, 78 wounded; filmed from multiple angles
Images of miners being shot while retreating circulate globally — first post-apartheid massacre of Black workers by Black government
Lonmin eventually grants 22% wage increase — partial demand met, far short of R12,500 demand
Cyril Ramaphosa (Lonmin board member, former NUM leader) faces intense public scrutiny for role in calling in police
Julius Malema founds Economic Freedom Fighters (EFF) directly on Marikana platform; wins parliament seats 2014
Farlam Commission report: criticizes police leadership but recommends no criminal prosecutions; effectively a whitewash
National Prosecuting Authority declines to prosecute police officers; no criminal accountability
Lonmin sold to Sibanye-Stillwater; eventually mine closed; workers who survived now unemployed
Marikana Memorial maintained; families still seeking accountability; Ramaphosa presidency shadowed by massacre
Mining sector wages remain inadequate relative to cost of living; AMCU vs NUM rivalry continues; no systemic change

24.what_actually_changed: A partial wage increase was granted. AMCU grew into a major mining union. The EFF was founded and is now a significant parliamentary force. Public consciousness about post-apartheid inequality was permanently sharpened — the myth of post-apartheid as a complete economic transformation was definitively punctured. However, no police officers were held criminally accountable, no systemic change to mining sector wage structure occurred, and Lonmin eventually closed. Ramaphosa became president despite his role — illustrating the limits of moral accountability in South African politics.

25.name: Liberia Peace Women / Leymah Gbowee
25.outcome_summary: Charles Taylor was forced from power, a peace deal was signed, Ellen Johnson Sirleaf was elected (first female African head of state), and Leymah Gbowee won the Nobel Peace Prize. One of the clearest cases in modern history of direct nonviolent action by women changing the outcome of a violent conflict. The movement achieved every goal.

progress_markers.event: First Liberian Civil War begins under Charles Taylor; 250,000+ killed over 14 years of intermittent conflict
Second Civil War begins; Taylor's forces committing atrocities; women and children primary victims of sexual violence
Leymah Gbowee organizes Women in Peacebuilding Network (WIPNET); brings Muslim and Christian women together under shared identity as mothers
Women begin prayer vigils in white T-shirts at Monrovia fish market — white = purity + visibility; sex strike announced as pressure tactic
April 2003: 'sex strike' gains international media attention; Taylor summoned women to State House; they hand him a peace petition
Peace talks in Ghana, July 2003: Women's delegation surrounds the building; when talks stall they chain themselves in the hall
Gbowee threatens to invoke traditional 'bad luck' through disrobing — powerful cultural curse when mothers direct it at men; negotiators return to table
August 2003: Taylor agrees to exile in Nigeria; peace deal signed; second civil war ends
November 2005: Ellen Johnson Sirleaf elected president — first female head of state in Africa
Taylor indicted by Special Court for Sierra Leone for war crimes; arrested when he attempts return
Leymah Gbowee wins Nobel Peace Prize (shared with Ellen Johnson Sirleaf and Tawakkol Karman of Yemen)
Charles Taylor convicted by Special Court for Sierra Leone — first sitting head of state convicted by international court since Nuremberg
Ellen Johnson Sirleaf's presidency ends amid corruption allegations; George Weah elected; women's representation drops in government
Liberia remains fragile but peaceful; Women in Peacebuilding Network continues; Gbowee's model replicated globally

25.what_actually_changed: Charles Taylor was removed from power, exiled, indicted, and ultimately convicted of war crimes — a complete accountability arc. A peace deal was signed that held. Ellen Johnson Sirleaf was elected as Africa's first female head of state. Leymah Gbowee's model of cross-religious women's nonviolent peacebuilding has been replicated in conflict zones from Syria to South Sudan. Liberia has maintained fragile but real peace since 2003 — now more than 20 years. The Women of Liberia Mass Action for Peace changed the outcome of a civil war, making it one of the most documented and complete examples of nonviolent women's action producing historic political change.

slogans: We want peace, no more war
key_media: Documentary: Pray the Devil Back to Hell (2008)
Leymah Gbowee: Mighty Be Our Powers (memoir)

sources.title: Women of Liberia Mass Action for Peace

26.name: Taiwan Sunflower Movement
26.outcome_summary: The CSSTA trade agreement was indefinitely shelved—a clear win on the immediate demand. New cross-strait oversight legislation was passed. The movement catalyzed a generation of civic activists, several of whom entered formal politics through the New Power Party. The KMT lost the 2016 election partly due to youth political awakening. However, the longer-term structural issue—Taiwan's democratic sovereignty under pressure from Beijing—remains existential and unresolved.

progress_markers.event: Ma Ying-jeou elected on cross-strait economic normalization platform; KMT begins Economic Cooperation Framework Agreement with China
ECFA (Economic Cooperation Framework Agreement) signed with China; critics warn of increasing economic dependence on Beijing
Ma Ying-jeou re-elected; youth political disillusionment growing among DPP-leaning urban youth
Cross-Strait Services Trade Agreement (CSSTA) negotiations completed; critics warn it opens sensitive service sectors to Chinese capital without adequate public debate
February-March: student groups and civic organizations form to oppose the CSSTA; Black Island Nation Youth Front mobilizes campus opposition
March 18: KMT committee chair passes CSSTA in under 60 seconds without clause-by-clause review; students storm and occupy Legislative Yuan
March 30: 500,000 march outside Legislative Yuan—largest protest in Taiwan history at that point; public support for students strong
April 6: Legislative Yuan Speaker Wang Jin-pyng promises to pass cross-strait oversight bill before reviewing CSSTA; students agree to leave
April 10: Occupation ends; CSSTA indefinitely shelved; Ma government agrees to oversight legislation requirement
Lin Fei-fan and other leaders launch civic organizations; Chen Wei-ting joins Freddy Lim's music-to-politics networks
New Power Party (NPP) founded by Sunflower leaders including Huang Kuo-chang and musician Freddy Lim
DPP wins presidential and legislative elections; NPP wins 5 legislative seats—first direct electoral conversion of movement energy
Cross-Strait Oversight Bill framework formally confirmed as legislative priority; CSSTA remains unratified
Hong Kong protests amplify Taiwan's concerns about 'one country, two systems'; Taiwan identity consciousness strengthened further
DPP wins third consecutive presidential election (Lai Ching-te); CSSTA has never been ratified; Taiwan civic engagement among youth remains high

27.name: Sri Lanka Aragalaya
27.outcome_summary: The Aragalaya successfully removed the Rajapaksa family from power—a historic achievement. However, the Parliament that replaced Gotabaya with Ranil Wickremesinghe (a Rajapaksa loyalist) implemented harsh IMF-mandated austerity. The new constitution that protesters demanded was never drafted. In 2024, Anura Kumara Dissanayake (NPP/JVP) won the presidency partly on the Aragalaya's legacy—an indirect but significant political shift. The economic crisis that caused the Aragalaya remains only partially resolved.

progress_markers.event: Gotabaya Rajapaksa elected on nationalist platform; implements economic mismanagement including fertilizer ban and tax cuts depleting reserves
COVID-19 destroys tourism (Sri Lanka's major forex earner); foreign reserves collapse from $7.5 billion to near zero
Overnight ban on chemical fertilizers destroys rice harvest; food prices surge; Rajapaksa family ignores expert warnings
March: Pharmacies empty of medicines; fuel queues stretch 3km; 13-hour daily power cuts; foreign debt default announced
March 31: Spontaneous gathering outside Gotabaya's private residence without party organization—'GotaGoHome' emerges
GotaGoGama protest camp outside Presidential Secretariat grows into parallel community with library, medical tent, free food, yoga—cross-ethnic, cross-religious
May 9: PM Mahinda Rajapaksa resigns after pro-government thugs attack protest camp; Wickremesinghe appointed PM
July 9: Hundreds of thousands storm Presidential Palace; Gotabaya Rajapaksa flees to Maldives then Singapore
July 14: Gotabaya Rajapaksa resigns—first president in Sri Lankan history removed by popular protest
July 20: Parliament elects Ranil Wickremesinghe as president—seen as establishment continuity candidate, not reform
July 22: Military raids GotaGoGama, forcibly removes protesters; opposition leaders arrested under PTA (Prevention of Terrorism Act)
IMF $2.9 billion bailout secured under harsh austerity conditions; electricity tariffs tripled; VAT increased; civil service wages cut
Sri Lanka settles external debt with bilateral creditors; economy stabilizes but poverty rates remain high
September: Anura Kumara Dissanayake (AKD/NPP) wins presidential election—first left-wing president in Sri Lankan history; NPP wins parliamentary supermajority in November
AKD government attempts to renegotiate IMF terms and prosecute Rajapaksa-era corruption; accountability for protest crackdown pending

28.name: Thailand Student Democracy Movement
28.outcome_summary: The movement achieved no structural reforms. Constitutional demands were rejected; calls for monarchy reform were ruled unconstitutional by the Constitutional Court. Hundreds of protesters faced lèse-majesté prosecutions. The 2023 elections showed the movement's cultural impact—progressive Move Forward party won the most seats—but was blocked from forming government and its leader subsequently banned. Thailand's democratic regression continued under Prayuth and his successor Srettha Thavisin.

progress_markers.event: Military coup (NCPO) overthrows elected government; Prayuth Chan-o-cha seizes power; political opposition driven underground
Junta manipulates 2019 election through constitutional engineering; Prayuth 'wins'; youth anger at rigged transition intensifies
February: Future Forward Party dissolved by Constitutional Court; student 'flash mobs' erupt at universities across Thailand
March-July: COVID-19 suppresses protests temporarily; economic pain from pandemic deepens grievances
August 10: Student protest at Democracy Monument; first public call for monarchy reform in modern Thai history by Panusaya Sithijirawattanakul ('Rung') reading 10-point manifesto
Three-finger Hunger Games salute adopted across Thai protest movement; Harry Potter and Hamtaro memes used to critique monarchy without explicit lèse-majesté violations
October 14-21: Mass protests; state of emergency declared in Bangkok; water cannons with dye used against protesters at Patumwan intersection
Constitutional Court rules that monarchy reform demands constitute attempt to 'overthrow' the constitutional monarchy—essentially criminalizing the movement's core demand
Mass lèse-majesté charges filed; Arnon Nampa, Parit Chiwarak, Jatupat Boonpattararaksa among hundreds charged; many held in pretrial detention
Hunger strikes by imprisoned activists draw international attention; UN Special Rapporteurs condemn prosecutions
1,634+ charged under various laws (lèse-majesté, sedition, COVID regulations); movement activists face decade-long legal battles
May elections: Move Forward party wins most seats (151/500); shows movement's cultural/electoral impact
Move Forward blocked from forming government by military-appointed Senate; Pheu Thai forms coalition with military parties instead
Move Forward renamed People's Party after Constitutional Court dissolution; leader Pita Limjaroenrat banned from politics for 10 years
Lèse-majesté prosecutions continue; People's Party remains largest opposition; structural reforms remain blocked by constitutional design

29.name: Japan Anti-Nuclear Movement
29.outcome_summary: The movement achieved its immediate goal—all 50 reactors shut down by 2013. But the Abe government reversed course from 2015 onwards, restarting reactors despite sustained public opposition. As of 2024, approximately 12 reactors are operational with more restarts planned. The movement demonstrated that Japan's democratic culture can mobilize mass public dissent for the first time in decades, but could not ultimately override energy security imperatives when economic and geopolitical pressures (post-Ukraine energy crisis) aligned against them.

progress_markers.event: March 11: Magnitude 9.0 earthquake and tsunami triggers Fukushima Daiichi triple meltdown; 150,000 evacuated from 20km exclusion zone
March-June: Government radiation data revealed as systematically understated; independent monitoring shows exclusion zone too small; public betrayal narrative emerges
July: PM Naoto Kan orders stress tests for all nuclear plants; 35 of 54 reactors offline by end of 2011
September 19: 60,000-person anti-nuclear rally at Meiji Shrine complex—first significant political mass protest in Japan in decades
May 5: Last operating reactor (Tomari Unit 3) shut down; Japan briefly achieves 'zero nuclear'
Sayonara Genpatsu petition reaches 7.5 million signatures—largest petition in Japanese history; Nobel laureate Kenzaburo Oe leads protests
July: 200,000 Friday night protests outside PM's residence in Tokyo—largest in decades; cross-generational participation
September: DPJ government announces 'nuclear-free by 2030s' policy; new Nuclear Regulation Authority established
December: Abe LDP wins election; explicitly reverses nuclear phase-out and plans reactor restarts
Abe government includes 20-22% nuclear in energy mix by 2030 in draft energy plan; nuclear restart process begins
August: Sendai reactor restarted—first in four years; nuclear restart program begins under new NRA safety standards
2011 Kumamoto earthquakes reignite safety fears but do not halt restart program; courts occasionally block specific restarts
Russia-Ukraine war causes energy crisis; Kishida government announces acceleration of reactor restarts and potential new plant construction
GX (Green Transformation) policy approved; Japan reverses decades of post-Fukushima direction; long-term operation extensions approved
12+ reactors operational; public opposition remains high (60%+) but overridden by energy security policy; decommissioning of older reactors continues

30.name: Pakistan Aurat March
30.outcome_summary: The Aurat March has not produced specific legislation but has fundamentally shifted the public discourse around women's rights in Pakistan. The annual march continues despite violent threats and legal challenges. 'Mera jism meri marzi' (My body my choice) became one of the most contested political slogans in Pakistani history. The Domestic Violence Act was passed in Sindh (2013, strengthened) and Punjab (2021) partly in this movement's wake. However, femicide rates remain high, honor killing protections remain weak, and the movement faces intensifying conservative backlash.

progress_markers.event: Women's Action Forum founded in response to Zia ul-Haq's Islamization laws; Pakistan's first organized feminist movement
Malala Yousafzai wins Nobel Peace Prize; global attention on Pakistani women's rights but limited domestic political change
Qandeel Baloch (social media star) murdered in 'honor killing' by brother; murder broadcast on social media creates national shock
Parliament passes Anti-Honor Killing law (Criminal Law Amendment Act)—first explicit criminalization of 'honor' as defense for murder
#MeToo global wave reaches Pakistan; Pakistani women begin speaking publicly about harassment in universities and workplaces
March 8: First Aurat March in Karachi, Lahore, Islamabad—International Women's Day marches explicitly framed as feminist
Second Aurat March; 'Mera jism meri marzi' poster triggers national controversy; religious groups and PTI politicians condemn march; FIRs (police complaints) filed
Backlash paradoxically amplifies movement nationally and internationally; diaspora Pakistanis join globally; march becomes annual fixture
Imran Khan government attempts to frame Aurat March as 'anti-Islamic' and 'Western-funded'; High Court petitions to ban march; courts reject bans
Punjab Domestic Violence Act passed with stronger provisions than previous law
Fifth Aurat March; largest ever; 'Inquilab aurat ka' (Women's revolution) becomes new rallying cry
Multiple court challenges to march permits; violence against some participants; Imran Khan-affiliated religious groups organize counter-marches
Aurat March continues; no major national legislation; discourse shift measurable in media coverage and political language around women's issues

31.name: Myanmar Civil Disobedience Movement
31.outcome_summary: The CDM was crushed in urban areas by sustained military violence. By mid-2021, over 1,000 protesters had been killed and thousands imprisoned. The junta maintained control of major cities. The pro-democracy resistance morphed into an armed struggle (People's Defence Force) that has achieved significant territorial control in ethnic areas by 2024-2025, but at enormous civilian cost. Myanmar is in civil war with no near-term path to democratic resolution. The CDM spirit continues in resistance zones but the non-violent movement was definitively suppressed.

progress_markers.event: Partial democratic opening under Thein Sein government; Aung San Suu Kyi released from house arrest; NLD participates in politics
NLD wins landslide election; Suu Kyi effectively leads government as State Counsellor; military retains 25% of parliamentary seats by constitutional design
November: NLD wins second landslide election; military claims fraud but election monitors find results credible
February 1 (pre-dawn): Military arrests Suu Kyi, President Win Myint, and elected government members; coup announced on state TV
February 2-5: Civil Disobedience Movement (CDM) launches; doctors, nurses, teachers, civil servants refuse to work for junta—largest professional strike in Myanmar's history
Three-finger salute (borrowed from Thai protesters) adopted; daily street protests with hundreds of thousands; international community condemns coup
February 28: Junta uses lethal force; first protesters killed; UN Special Envoy Thomas Andrews reports security forces firing on crowds
March-April: Systematic campaign of arrests, torture, killings; internet shutdowns; crackdowns intensify; protests driven underground in cities
May: People's Defence Force (PDF) formed by CRPH; armed resistance begins; ethnic armed organizations ally with pro-democracy forces
Junta executes four prominent democracy activists including Ko Jimmy and Phyo Zeya Thaw—first executions in Myanmar in decades
Civil war expands; PDF and ethnic armed groups control significant territory in Sagaing, Magway, Chin State, Kayah
Operation 1027: Brotherhood Alliance offensive seizes 200+ military outposts in Shan State; major territorial defeats for junta
Major towns in multiple states fall to resistance forces; junta territory significantly reduced; military under severe pressure
Civil war continues; junta controls Naypyidaw and major cities; NUG (National Unity Government) administers resistance areas; outcome uncertain
